coredata.dc:title: Design of few-layered 1T-MoS2 by supramolecular-assisted assembly with N-doped carbon quantum dots for supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Kemeng
Luo, Zhongwei
Shu, Dong
Yi, Fenyun
Zhu, Zhenhua
Gao, Aimei
coredata.dc:description: 1T phase molybdenum disulfide decorated by N-doped carbon quantum dots (MoS2/NCQDs) is successfully synthesized by supramolecular-assisted method. N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) derived from citric acid and thiourea can form a supramolecular structure through hydrogen bonding, which spurs the formation process of MoS2 at a molecular level. The results demonstrate that NCQDs in supramolecular system and NH3 coming from thiourea effectively prevent the agglomeration of MoS2, resulting in the formation of 1T-MoS2 with a wide interlayer spacing about 0.95 nm. The as-prepared MoS2/NCQDs-6 possesses an excellent supercapacitive performance (379.5F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1) and outstanding rate capability (269.7F g−1 at 10 A g−1) under the synergy of NCQDs and 1T phase MoS2. The fabricated flexible asymmetric supercapacitor delivers an optimal energy density of 33.5 Wh kg−1 at 722.0 W kg−1. The excellent supercapacitive response should be contributed to the wide interlayer spacing of 1T-MoS2 which can improve the faster ion diffusion and the introduction of NCQDs which can offer a good electrical conductivity and enhance the hydrophilic property of the material. This work illumines a simple effective way to fabricate practical used 1T MoS2 hybrid electrode materials for supercapacitor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
1T phase molybdenum disulfide
Supramolecular method
Flexible asymmetric supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NCQDs doped 1T-2H phase molybdenum disulfide (MoS2/NCQDs) and pure MoS2

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to acknowledge the following financial supporter of this work: the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21673086).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the synthesis of MoS2/NCQDs.

(a) SEM images of MoS2, (b) MoS2/NCQDs-4, (c) MoS2/NCQDs-6 and (d) MoS2/NCQDs-8; (e, f) HRTEM images of MoS2/NCQDs-6, (g) Overall low-magnification TEM image of MoS2/NCQDs-6, (h-l) Corresponding elemental mapping images of (g).
(a) XRD spectra of MoS2, MoS2/NCQDs composites, (b) Raman spectroscopy of MoS2 and MoS2/NCQDs-6, (c),(d) contact angles of MoS2 and MoS2/NCQDs-6.
XPS spectra of (a) Mo 3d, (b) S 2p, (c) C 1s and (d) N 1s core level peak regions of the synthesized CQDs/MoS2-6; the curves are deconvoluted by Gauss fitting.

(a) CV and (b) GCD curves of all samples at a voltage scan rate of 50 mV s−1 and a current density of 1 A g−1 in 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. (c) Specific capacitances of MoS2, MoS2/NCQDs-4, MoS2/NCQDs-6 and MoS2/NCQDs-8 composites at different current densities of 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 7 and 10 A g−1. (d) CV and (e) GCD curves of MoS2/NCQDs-6 in 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte at various current densities and scan rates. (f) Nyquist plots of all samples, (g) Cycle performance of the MoS2/NCQDs-6 at 50 mV s−1 scan rate for 5000 cycles.

(a) Schematic illustration of the fabricated ASC, (b) Comparative CV curves (20 mV s−1) of MnO2 and MoS2/NCQDs-6 electrodes, (c) CV curves of MnO2//MoS2/NCQDs-6 asymmetric supercapacitor device at different scan rates, (d) GCD curves at different current densities, (e) CV curve of flexible device bending 0°, 90° and 180° at a sweep speed of 20 mV s−1, (f) Ragone plot of the as-fabricated asymmetric capacitors, (g) Cycling performances of MnO2//MoS2/NCQDs-6 asymmetric supercapacitor device; (h) A picture exhibiting two MnO2//MoS2/NCQDs-6 supercapacitors in series can light up a LED indicator.

head.ce:title: Design of few-layered 1T-MoS2 by supramolecular-assisted assembly with N-doped carbon quantum dots for supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Materials and Technology for Electrochemical Energy Storage (Ministry of Education), PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Materials and Technology for Electrochemical Energy Storage (Ministry of Education)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Materials and Technology for Electrochemical Energy Storage (Ministry of Education), PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 1T phase molybdenum disulfide decorated by N-doped carbon quantum dots (MoS2/NCQDs) is successfully synthesized by supramolecular-assisted method. N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) derived from citric acid and thiourea can form a supramolecular structure through hydrogen bonding, which spurs the formation process of MoS2 at a molecular level. The results demonstrate that NCQDs in supramolecular system and NH3 coming from thiourea effectively prevent the agglomeration of MoS2, resulting in the formation of 1T-MoS2 with a wide interlayer spacing about 0.95 nm. The as-prepared MoS2/NCQDs-6 possesses an excellent supercapacitive performance (379.5F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1) and outstanding rate capability (269.7F g−1 at 10 A g−1) under the synergy of NCQDs and 1T phase MoS2. The fabricated flexible asymmetric supercapacitor delivers an optimal energy density of 33.5 Wh kg−1 at 722.0 W kg−1. The excellent supercapacitive response should be contributed to the wide interlayer spacing of 1T-MoS2 which can improve the faster ion diffusion and the introduction of NCQDs which can offer a good electrical conductivity and enhance the hydrophilic property of the material. This work illumines a simple effective way to fabricate practical used 1T MoS2 hybrid electrode materials for supercapacitor.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous development of technology and the increasing demand of energy, the search for clean and green energy is becoming significantly important for the healthy development of the world [1–3]. Supercapacitors, also known as electrochemical capacitors [4], have appealed great interest due to the high current discharge capacity, super power density and excellent cycling stability (>100,000 cycles) [5,6]. According to the energy storage mechanism, the electrode materials of supercapacitors can be divided into: surface electrostatic adsorption type materials, namely electric double layer capacitor (EDLC), pseudocapacitive type materials based on surface redox reaction, and battery type materials based on surface and bulk redox reaction [7]. As one of the promising negative electrode supercapacitor materials, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), a typical transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), has achieved widespread interest [8,9].

MoS2 features a graphene-like two-dimensional (2D) layered structure in which one Mo atom layer and two atomic sulfur layers (S-Mo-S) are formed a sandwiched structure through covalent bonding, while the interlayers of MoS2 are bonded together by van der Waals interaction [10–12]. Basically, MoS2 exists in two distinct crystalline phases, that is, 2H phase and 1T phase. For 2H phase MoS2 displaying a trigonal prismatic structure, it is a quite thermodynamically stable material and easily to be synthesized. Nevertheless, its conductivity is not far from satisfactory for the energy storage devices. Therefore, the combination of MoS2 with carbon materials has become an efficient approach to promote the conductivity and prevent agglomeration of MoS2 [13–16]. For example, Kumuthini et al. reported a specific capacitance of 355.6 F g−1 at the scan rate of 5 mV s−1 in 1 M Na2SO4 for carbon nanofibers coated MoS2 [17]; Liu et al. reported a material of MoS2 coated on hollow carbon spheres by solvothermal method, which can get a specific capacitance of 334 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 in 3 M KOH [18]. On the other hand, 1T-phase MoS2 displays a trigonal antiprismatic structure with a metallic character. This structure benefits its electron transport and ion diffusion, thus 1T-phase MoS2 has appealed a lot of attention in electrochemistry and energy storage fields [19,20]. Nevertheless, the synthesis of 1T MoS2 is very difficult through the common chemical methods. A traditional method to achieve 1T-MoS2 is to use lithium-ion intercalation in organic solvent, which is quite dangerous if exposed to air. Therefore, in order to obtain 1T-MoS2 or 1T-2H heterostructure MoS2 material, lots of efforts have been made. Wei et al. reported that introducing sulfur vacancies (VS) into the phase of 2H MoS2 can prompt the 2H-MoS2 lattice to transform into 1T-MoS2, which can increase the electron carrier concentration greatly [21]. Through solvothermal growth in which N,N-Dimethylformamide served as a controlling agent and an insertion agent, Pan et al. synthesized 1T MoS2 nanosheet on the surface of carbon fiber for Li-ion battery [22]. The electrochemical performance of this 1T MoS2 nanosheet was superior to that of 2H MoS2 material. Group of Ye described that using lithium molten salts at different temperatures, they achieved various phases of MoS2 (2H, 1T or 3R phase) for hydrogen evolution. When the temperature increased higher than 1000 °C, the 1T-phase MoS2 could be gained [23]. Owing to the intrinsic conducting character and large layered space, it is still a hot topic to investigate 1T MoS2 or a 1T-2H hybrid phases for supercapacitor material.

As a typical zero dimensional carbon material, carbon quantum dots (GQDs) exhibit good hydrophilicity and excellent electrical conductivity [24]. Furthermore, GQDs also can serve as an intercalator to effectively restrain the restack of 2D layered materials [25]. Inspired by the excellent property of GQDs, 1T phase MoS2 modified by N-doped CQDs (MoS2/NCQDs) is simply synthesized through supramolecular assisted method in this work. The prepared NCQDs with the uniform size of 3.5 nm, bearing multiple oxygen-containing functional groups, can shape a supramolecular structure with thiourea through hydrogen bonding, and thus spur the formation process of 1T MoS2 at a molecular level. The results show that MoS2 in NCQDs/MoS2 composites exhibit 1T-2H hybrid phase and enlarged interlayer spacing which allows electrolyte ions to transfer rapidly, thus showing excellent electrochemical performance.
Sodium molybdate (AR, Na2MoO4·2H2O), thiourea (AR, CH4N2S), Urea ((NH2)2CO, 99%) and ethyl alcohol (CH3CH2OH, 99.7%) were provided by ZhiYuan Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin).

Typically, 0.42 g of citric acid and 0.36 g of urea were added into 20 mL of deionized water, then reacted at 160 ℃ for 4 h in the Tefion-lined autoclave. After dialysis and freeze-drying treatment, the N-doped CQDs were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NCQDs doped 1T-2H phase molybdenum disulfide (MoS2/NCQDs) and pure MoS2
ce:section.ce:para: After different amount of carbon quantum dots (20 mg, 30 mg and 40 mg) were dissolved in 40 mL deionized water, 1.22 g of thiourea and 0.78 g of sodium molybdate was added in these NCQDs solution in turn and stirred for 40 min, respectively. The mixtures were transferred into the sealed autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 10 h. After washed and dried, the prepared products were recorded as MoS2/NCQDs-4, MoS2/NCQDs-6 and MoS2/NCQDs-8. The pure MoS2 material was prepared as the similar process without adding NCQDs.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) test was carried out on D2-phaser diffractometer. The magnified morphology of the material was explored using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements on ZEISS Ultra-55. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements combined with mapping were probed on JEM-2100HR. Raman spectrometers of MoS2 and CQDs/MoS2 were performed using Jobin-Yvon Lab-RAM HR800. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of materials was obtained on a K-Alpha + X-ray photoelectron spectrometer.

The electrochemical properties of pure MoS2 and MoS2/NCQDs materials were investigated on the electrochemical work station (CHI 660E). For the three-electrode setup in 1 M Na2SO4, pure MoS2 or MoS2/NCQDs, PVDF and acetylene black are mixed together in a mass ratio about 8:1:1 and coated on a stainless steel mesh (1*1 cm2) to prepare a working electrode. The mass of the active materials is approximately 2.0 mg cm−2. The reference electrode and counter electrode was Hg/Hg2Cl2 and Pt foil, respectively. For the asymmetric two-electrode supercapacitor device testing, MnO2 (about 4.1 mg) was used as the positive electrode, MoS2/NCQDs (about 2.2 mg) as the negative electrode material. The synthesis details of MnO2 were provided in supporting information. Electrolyte was prepared by mixing 1.5 g of Na2SO4 and 2 g of PVP in 30 mL of DI H2O. The separator is a piece of cellulose paper.

The preparation process of MoS2/NCQDs is exhibited in Fig. 1 . The obtained NCQDs with an average size of about 3.5 nm (Figure S1a and b) show a distinct (002) XRD peak near 26° (Figure S1c) similar to the graphite structure [26]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of NCQDs displays a characteristic absorption peak at around 338 nm (Figure S1e) [27]. Under 365 nm ultraviolet light illumination, NCQDs solution successfully emits blue luminescence (Figure S1h), which further exhibits the character of carbon quantum dots. The photoluminescence of NCQDs should be related to the sp2 carbon (Figure S1d) about few nanometres isolated by the sp3 CO structure, implying the presence of multiple oxygen-containing functional groups [28]. The –NH2 of thiourea and the oxygen-containing functional groups of NCQDs can combine with each other through hydrogen bonding [29,30] and form a supramolecular structure. Therefore, the formed supramolecule can affect the formation process of MoS2 at a molecular level. That is, during the hydrothermal process, the thiourea in the supramolecular system will turn into the reacting center with molybdate ion, and then molybdenum disulfide nanosheets can be formed gradually directed by this kind of supramolecular system. Furthermore, the formed supramolecule also can serve as intercalators and then facilitate the transformation of trigonal prismatic structure 2H-MoS2 to trigonal antiprismatic structure 1T-MoS2 (Figure S2). Of course, NH3 coming from thiourea may also insert into the intelayers of MoS2 and improve the shaping of 1T MoS2. With the growing of MoS2 nanosheets, the carbon quantum dots in the supramolecular system can anchor on MoS2 sheet, which can further serve as an intercalator to effectively prevent the aggregation of MoS2 nanosheets [25].

In order to explore the morphology of the as-prepared pristine MoS2 and composite materials, SEM is performed, as shown in Fig. 2 . It can be seen that both pure molybdenum disulfide and MoS2/NCQDs composite materials have a flower-like structure which will be in favor of the ion diffusion between the electrolyte and the material. The diameter of pure MoS2 (Fig. 2a) is about 1 μm; after combined with the carbon quantum dots, the size of all MoS2/NCQDs composites significantly decreased to about 200 nm. Furthermore, the edge of the composite materials, especially MoS2/NCQDs-6 in Fig. 2c, appears to have a thinner lamellar structure than that of pure MoS2. The results of SEM imply that carbon quantum dots and the formed supramolecule are favorable for preventing the accumulation of molybdenum disulfide, which thus can boost the electrochemical performance of MoS2/NCQDs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is further conducted to test the composite of MoS2/NCQDs-6. Apparently, the molybdenum disulfide layer in MoS2/NCQDs-6 is very thin (Fig. 2e), which benefits from the barrier effect of supramolecular system at a molecular level during preparation. It also can be found a lot of black dots anchored on the surface of MoS2/NCQDs-6 thin layer that should be the carbon quantum dots in the composite. Many lattice fringes of MoS2/NCQDs-6 can be observed in high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 2f), and the interplanar spacing is about 0.95 nm which is much larger than that of (002) crystal plane of 2H-MoS2. The larger interlayer spacing should be the property of the (002) lattice plane of the 1T phase MoS2 [31]. The disordered and loose lattice fringes in Fig. 2f are another obvious phenomenon, which implies that MoS2/NCQDs-6 compound possesses a few-layered structure. This unique structure will be prone to elevate the surface area and be favorable for the ion transportation. All the above results should be primarily due to the embedding of carbon quantum dots in prepared MoS2/NCQDs-6 or the generation of NH3 from the decomposition of thiourea which can serve as intercalators and facilitate the formation of 1T MoS2. The elemental mapping images of MoS2/NCQDs-6 are exhibited in Fig. 2g-l. As expected, elements of Mo, S, C, O and N are detected uniformly. Mo and S should be assigned to MoS2, element C corresponds to NCQDs. Element N comes from the decomposition of urea or thiourea during the synthesis procedure of material. The presence of the N element indicates that N element is successfully doped into the material. Element O is attributed to the oxygen-containing group of NCQDs. These results further indicate that NCQDs are uniformly anchored on the molybdenum disulfide.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) of pure MoS2 and composite materials is shown in Fig. 3 a. For pure MoS2, diffraction peaks at 2θ = 14.12°, 32.7° and 57.60° are the characteristic signals of (002), (100) and (110) planes of 2H-MoS2, respectively [32]. Interestingly, for MoS2/NCQDs composites, the (002) peak centered at 14.12° becomes weak and even disappears, and a new peak at 9.64° emerges, which means that the MoS2/NCQDs possess an enlarged interlayer spacing among the layered structures. The new diffraction peaks at 2θ = 9.64° should be indexed to (002) plane of the 1T-MoS2 [33]. This result indicates that 1T-2H heterostructure of MoS2 in composite materials are synthesized successfully, implying that the introduction of NCQDs and supramolecular system should improve the forming of 1T phase MoS2.

Raman spectroscopy is utilized to further recognize the 1T phase MoS2 in MoS2/NCQDs-6, as exhibited in Fig. 3b. For pure MoS2, the characteristic peaks lie at 283, 379 and 404 cm−1 belonging to E1g, E1 2g and A1g of 2H MoS2, respectively. For the MoS2/NCQDs-6 composite, new strong Raman bands at 221.46 and 335.78 cm−1 are clearly observed, which arise from the J2 and J3 vibration modes of S–Mo–S in 1T MoS2 phase [34], further suggesting the formation of 1T- MoS2 in the sample of MoS2/NCQDs-6. The result is consistent to that of XRD. The D band and G band of carbon quantum dots are also recognized at 1362.33 cm−1 and 1604.25 cm−1, demonstrating the successful combination of carbon quantum dots with molybdenum disulfide.

The static contact angle results of MoS2 and MoS2/NCQDs-6 are shown in Fig. 3c and d, 103.5° for MoS2 and 87.5° for MoS2/NCQDs-6. It is obvious that the contact angle of MoS2/NCQDs-6 is lower than that of pure MoS2, indicating that the wetting ability of MoS2/NCQDs-6 material is improved significantly due to the existence of NCQDs, 1T MoS2 and oxygen-containing groups. So, it can be expected that MoS2/NCQDs-6 should achieve a higher electrolyte uptake ability [35] and a boosted electrochemical performance. The introduction of carbon quantum dots also can increase the number of mesopores and macropores and enlarge the specific surface area of MoS2/NCQDs (Figure S3 in supporting information).

The elemental composition of the synthesized MoS2/NCQDs-6 is further studied using X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). The Mo 3d spectrum of MoS2/NCQDs-6 is displayed in Fig. 4 a. It can be found two peaks at 229.7 and 232.9 eV which belong to Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2 of 2H-MoS2. The other two peaks at 228.9 and 232.1 eV are the property peaks of Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 in 1T-MoS2, respectively [36]. The bonding energy of Mo 3d spectrum in 1T-MoS2 is slightly lower than that in 2H-MoS2. It suggests that the 1T phase MoS2 exists in this sample [36] like the analysis of XRD and Raman spectra. The 3d3/2 peak of Mo6+ locates at 235.8 eV, implying that MoOC bonds are formed at the interphase between MoS2 and carbon quantum dots [22]. The S 2p spectrum in Fig. 4b also can be found the S 2p1/2 (163.2 eV) and S 2p3/2 (161.5 eV) peaks of 1T-MoS2 [37], exhibiting a lower bonding energy tendency compared with those of 2H-MoS2. The peak at 284.8 eV of C 1s in Fig. 4c mainly ascribes to CC; while the C 1s peaks due to CO/COMo and CO locates at 286.1 eV and 287.1 eV, respectively, further indicating the existence of MoOC bonds and successful combination of MoS2 with NCQDs. N 1s peak displays three characteristic peaks at about 398, 400.1 and 401.8 eV (Fig. 4d), attributing to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and quaternary nitrogen, respectively [38], which clearly demonstrates that N element is successfully doped into composite materials.. The existence of quaternary nitrogen is beneficial to improve the electrical conductivity of materials. Overall, the results of XPS strongly prove that 1T-2H phase molybdenum disulfide in MoS2/NCQDs-6 composite is successfully synthesized due to the introduction of NCQDs by supramolecular method. The supramolecular system between NCQDs and thiourea can contribute to shape 1T-MoS2 and preventing the accumulation of MoS2 at a molecular level. The introduction of NCQDs and NH3 coming from the decomposition of thiourea also can prefer the shaping of 1T-MoS2 or promote 2H-phase MoS2 transferring to 1T-MoS2, similar to the effect of lithium ion intercalation in organic solvent [25,39].

Considering the few-layer and 1T-2H MoS2 hybrid features, MoS2/NCQDs composites should be good potential electrode materials for supercapacitor. Therefore, a series of electrochemical measuring techniques are performed to assess the supercapacitor performance of the obtained samples. Fig. 5 a displays the CV curves of MoS2, MoS2/NCQDs-4, MoS2/NCQDs-6 and MoS2/NCQDs-8, examined at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. It can be viewed that the integrated area of all the composite materials increases compared with that of pure MoS2, disclosing that the introduction of NCQDs can improve the capacitance performance of the composites, especially MoS2/NCQDs-6 which achieves the largest integrated area. Appropriate amount of NCQDs can well prevent the restack of MoS2 and reduce the conductivity of material, which will promote the utilization of MoS2. The galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) curves of four samples at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 are given in Fig. 5b. All samples show a fairly standard triangular-shape, one of the typical characters of electrical double-layer capacitance. Their specific capacitance (Cg) of materials was calculated referring to the following formula:
Cg = It/mV (1)

The numerators I and t represent discharge current and discharge time, and the denominators m and V are electrode material weight and voltage changes respectively. The Cg of MoS2, MoS2/NCQDs-4, MoS2/NCQDs-6 and MoS2/NCQDs-8 at 0.5 A.g−1 are determined to be 146.5, 276.5, 379.5 and 219.0 F g−1, respectively. When the ratio of carbon dots is 6%, the composite MoS2/NCQDs-6 exhibits the best supercapacitor performance about 379.5 F g−1. Fig. 5c displays the rate performance of obtained materials at different current density from 0.5A g−1 to 10A g−1. As the current density raising to 10 A g−1, the Cg of MoS2, MoS2/NCQDs-4, MoS2/NCQDs-6 and MoS2/NCQDs-8 are 65.3, 149.1, 269.7 and 145.9 F g−1, respectively, and maintain 45%, 54%, 71% and 67% of each initial specific capacitance at 0.5A g−1. The results indicate MoS2/NCQDs-6 composite displays a perfect rate performance, owing to the disordered loose of 1T-2H heterostructure and the wide layer spacing of MoS2/NCQDs-6, which will facilitate electron transport and ion diffusion.

The CV curves of MoS2/NCQDs-6 under different scanning rates are carefully tested and given in the Fig. 5d. All CV profiles exhibit similar quasi-rectangular shape so far as to at a high scan rate of 200 mV s−1, which indicates an ideal double-layer capacitance performance and a good rate behavior [11,40]. All of the GCD curves of MoS2/NCQDs-6 from 0.5 to 10 A g−1 display a symmetrical triangular shape (Fig. 5e), indicating an excellent reversibility of MoS2/NCQDs-6.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscope (EIS) is performed to compare the capacitive traits of the electrode materials (Fig. 5f and Figure S4). Each impedance pattern is composed of a straight line and a semicircular architecture. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) at high-frequency is directly obtained according to the semicirculararc diameter. From the Nyquist plots (Fig. 5f) and the equivalent circuit model (Figure S4), the Rct of MoS2, MoS2/NCQDs-4, MoS2/NCQDs-6 and MoS2/NCQDs-8 are 4.15, 1.37, 0.68 and 0.86 Ω, respectively, and MoS2/NCQDs-6 demonstrates the lowest Rct which will benefit the supercapacitive performance. The Rct of the composites are significantly less than that of pure molybdenum disulfide, belonging to doped NCQDs, the hybrid structure of the 1T-2H phase. The presence of quaternary nitrogen in the composite also can enhance the electrical conductivity of material [41]. When the doping of NCQDs is too much, such as MoS2/NCQDs-8, NCQDs will pile up and affect the well disperse of MoS2 in the compound, and then enlarge the value of Rct. The cycling property of the MoS2/NCQDs-6 at 50 mV s−1 is explored (Fig. 5g). MoS2/NCQDs-6 sample displays a super cycling stability and remains 82% after 5000 cycles.

The above results imply that the obtained MoS2/NCQDs-6 is a quite good material for supercapacitor (Table S1). Therefore, an asymmetric supercapacitor device is assembled in which a manganese dioxide (MnO2) electrode is treated as a positive electrode, MoS2/NCQDs-6 electrode as a negative electrode, and 1 M PVP/Na2SO4 gel electrolyte, demonstrating in Fig. 6 a. According to the CV test at 20 mV s−1 (Fig. 6b), the two electrodes can operate stably and match well. The specific capacitance of MnO2 is about 179.3F g−1 and for MoS2/CQDs-6 is 333.5F g−1 at 20 mV s−1 (Fig. 6 b). Therefore, to balance the charge of the two electrodes according to Equation (2), the mass ratio m+ (positive electrode, about 4.1 mg) and m− (negative electrode, about 2.2 mg) is approximately 1.86.

The asymmetric supercapacitor displays a quasi-rectangular CV curve (Fig. 6c), and maintains the shape well even when the scan rate is increased to 100 mV s−1. The asymmetric supercapacitor obtains a high specific capacitance of 96.8F g−1 at a sweep rate of 5 mV s−1. Furthermore, the GCD profiles at different current densities exhibit series of symmetric charge/discharge curves shown in Fig. 6d, implying a perfect coulombic efficiency. The calculated gravimetric capacitance for our asymmetric supercapacitor is 83.5F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1, demonstrating the excellent capacitance performance of capacitors. The charge transfer resistance is about 2.04 Ω (Figure S5 a), indicating a low internal resistance for the whole device. The device has a specific capacitance of 83.5 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 and a specific capacitance of 42.5 F g−1 at 10 A g−1 (Figure S5 b). It exhibits the quite good rate performance of the device.

Fig. 6e shows cyclic voltammetry curves of the devices folded at 0°, 90° and 180° at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. Evidently, cyclic voltammetry does not occur changes at different angles of folding, indicating the stability and flexible character of the constructed asymmetric supercapacitor. Fig. 6f exhibits the Ragone diagram of the obtained device, and the values of energy density (E) and power density (P) were calculated based on Equations (3) and (4):
where ΔV is the potential window, and t is the discharging time.

The constructed asymmetric supercapacitor device exhibits a supreme energy density about 33.5 Wh kg−1 at 722 W kg−1. Compared with the previous work of asymmetric supercapacitors, our data still has certain advantages, such as NiCo2S4//C (22.8 Wh kg−1 at 160 W kg−1) [42], rGO-MoS2-WS2-4//rGO (15 Wh kg−1 at 372 W kg−1) [43], MoS2/GF//AEG (16Wh kg−1 at 758 W kg−1) [44] and MoS2@HCS//rGO (13.7 Wh kg−1 at 616 W kg−1) [45]. Meanwhile, long-term cycling stability of the asymmetric devices is performed at 5 mV s−1 (Fig. 6g). The capacitance retention is 90% of the initial capacitance after 2000 cycles, revealing an excellent cycling stability. Moreover, after connecting two asymmetric supercapacitors in series, it can illuminate an LED lamp (1.8 V) for nearly 6 min (Fig. 6h), implying great application prospects.

In conclusion, novel 1T-2H hybrid MoS2/NCQDs composites are deliberately fabricated through the supramolecular-induced method which can affect the formation of 1T-MoS2 at a molecular level. The 1T-2H phase MoS2 features a few layered structure with an ultra-wide space of 0.95 nm, which favors the diffusion of ions between electrode and electrolyte. The electrical conductivity and hydrophilic property of MoS2/NCQDs materials are improved greatly owing to the introduction of NCQDs and the formation of 1T phase MoS2. Therefore, MoS2/NCQDs-6 composite exhibits excellent electrochemical performance benefiting from the synergy of NCQDs and 1T phase MoS2. In three-electrode system, the MoS2/NCQDs-6 electrode exhibits high capacitance of 379.5 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 and remains 269.7 F g−1 (71%) as the current density increases to 10 A g−1, revealing its excellent rate capability. In addition, the asymmetric supercapacitor device fabricated by MnO2 (positive electrodes) and MoS2/NCQDs-6 (negative electrodes) reveals a satisfactory energy density of 33.5 Wh kg−1. Its cycling stability still displays 82% retention after 5000 cycles. Considering the unique structure and quite good supercapacitive performance of MoS2/NCQDs, this work is hoped to throw a little inspiration on the fabrication of high-performance 1T phase MoS2 for advanced energy storage devices.

Kemeng Yang: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Zhongwei Luo: Data curation, Formal analysis. Dong Shu: Project administration, Resources. Fenyun Yi: Conceptualization. Zhenhua Zhu: Validation. Aimei Gao: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge the following financial supporter of this work: the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21673086).
